import Game.Metadata

World "FinsetImage"
Level 4

Title "Image Grows with the Set"

Introduction "
# Larger Input, Larger Image

If $y \\in f(S)$, then certainly $y \\in f(S \\cup T)$ ‚Äî adding more
inputs to $f$ can only give more outputs.

This level combines **both directions** of `Finset.mem_image`:
- **Backward**: extract the witness from the hypothesis `y ‚àà s.image f`
- **Forward**: re-use the witness to prove `y ‚àà (s ‚à™ t).image f`

**Your task**: Given `h : y ‚àà s.image f`, prove `y ‚àà (s ‚à™ t).image f`.

Strategy: extract the witness `x` from `h` (with `x ‚àà s` and
`f x = y`), then provide the SAME witness for the larger set,
noting that `x ‚àà s` implies `x ‚àà s ‚à™ t`.
"

/-- If y is in the image of s, it's in the image of any superset. -/
Statement (f : ‚Ñï ‚Üí ‚Ñï) (s t : Finset ‚Ñï) (y : ‚Ñï) (h : y ‚àà s.image f) :
    y ‚àà (s ‚à™ t).image f := by
  Hint "First, extract the witness from `h`. Use
  `rw [Finset.mem_image] at h` to unwrap the existential."
  rw [Finset.mem_image] at h
  Hint "Now use `obtain ‚ü®x, hx, hfx‚ü© := h` to get the witness `x`,
  its membership `hx : x ‚àà s`, and `hfx : f x = y`."
  obtain ‚ü®x, hx, hfx‚ü© := h
  Hint "Now build the membership proof for the larger set.
  Use `rw [Finset.mem_image]` on the goal to convert it to
  an existential."
  rw [Finset.mem_image]
  Hint "Provide the SAME witness: `use x`."
  use x
  Hint "Split with `constructor`. For the membership part,
  `x ‚àà s` implies `x ‚àà s ‚à™ t` ‚Äî use `rw [Finset.mem_union]`
  and `left`."
  constructor
  ¬∑ Hint "Use `rw [Finset.mem_union]` to convert `x ‚àà s ‚à™ t`
    into `x ‚àà s ‚à® x ‚àà t`. Then `left` and `exact hx`."
    rw [Finset.mem_union]
    left
    exact hx
  ¬∑ Hint (hidden := true) "The functional equation hasn't changed.
    Use `exact hfx`."
    exact hfx

Conclusion "
This level showed the **extract-and-reuse** pattern:
1. Extract: `rw [mem_image] at h; obtain ‚ü®x, hx, hfx‚ü© := h`
2. Reuse: `rw [mem_image]; use x; constructor`

The witness `x` and its functional equation `f x = y` transfer
unchanged ‚Äî only the membership proof needs to be strengthened
from `x ‚àà s` to `x ‚àà s ‚à™ t`.

In plain math: if $x \\in S$ witnesses $y \\in f(S)$, then the same
$x$ witnesses $y \\in f(S \\cup T)$, since $S \\subseteq S \\cup T$.

This pattern arises whenever you need to show that image is
**monotone**: bigger input sets produce bigger images.
"

DisabledTactic trivial ¬´decide¬ª native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num by_cases tauto push_neg linarith
DisabledTheorem Finset.mem_insert_self Finset.mem_insert_of_mem Finset.mem_image_of_mem Finset.image_subset_image Finset.image_mono Finset.image_union Finset.image_subset_iff Finset.subset_union_left Finset.subset_union_right Finset.mem_union_left Finset.mem_union_right le_sup_left le_sup_right
import Game.Metadata

World "FinsetImage"
Level 5

Title "Image Distributes Over Union"

Introduction "
# Image and Union: One Direction

Level 4 showed that if $y \\in f(S)$, then $y \\in f(S \\cup T)$ ‚Äî a
larger input can only give more outputs. This level proves the
**reverse redistribution**: if $y \\in f(S \\cup T)$, then $y$ must
come from $f(S)$ or $f(T)$ (or both).

Together with Level 4, this establishes that image distributes
over union: $f(S \\cup T) = f(S) \\cup f(T)$.

**Your task**: Given `h : x ‚àà (s ‚à™ t).image f`, prove
`x ‚àà s.image f ‚à™ t.image f`.

Strategy:
1. Extract the witness `a` from `h` (backward extraction)
2. Determine whether `a ‚àà s` or `a ‚àà t` (case split on union)
3. Provide the same witness for the appropriate side
"

/-- If x is in the image of a union, it's in the union of the images. -/
Statement (f : ‚Ñï ‚Üí ‚Ñï) (s t : Finset ‚Ñï) (x : ‚Ñï)
    (h : x ‚àà (s ‚à™ t).image f) :
    x ‚àà s.image f ‚à™ t.image f := by
  Hint "Extract the witness from `h`. Use `rw [Finset.mem_image] at h`
  to unwrap the existential."
  rw [Finset.mem_image] at h
  Hint "Use `obtain ‚ü®a, ha, hfa‚ü© := h` to get the witness `a`,
  its membership `ha : a ‚àà s ‚à™ t`, and `hfa : f a = x`."
  obtain ‚ü®a, ha, hfa‚ü© := h
  Hint "Now `ha : a ‚àà s ‚à™ t`. Use `rw [Finset.mem_union] at ha`
  to split into `a ‚àà s ‚à® a ‚àà t`."
  rw [Finset.mem_union] at ha
  Hint "The goal is `x ‚àà s.image f ‚à™ t.image f`.
  Use `rw [Finset.mem_union]` to convert it to a disjunction,
  then case-split on `ha` with `cases ha with | inl hl => ... | inr hr => ...`."
  rw [Finset.mem_union]
  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `cases ha with` then handle each case
  using `left` or `right` and the forward membership pattern."
  cases ha with
  | inl hl =>
    Hint "In this case, `hl : a ‚àà s`. Use `left` to target `s.image f`,
    then provide the witness."
    left
    rw [Finset.mem_image]
    exact ‚ü®a, hl, hfa‚ü©
  | inr hr =>
    Hint "In this case, `hr : a ‚àà t`. Use `right` to target `t.image f`,
    then provide the witness."
    right
    rw [Finset.mem_image]
    exact ‚ü®a, hr, hfa‚ü©

Conclusion "
You've proved one direction: every element of $f(S \\cup T)$
belongs to $f(S) \\cup f(T)$. Combined with Level 4 (the other
direction), this establishes $f(S \\cup T) = f(S) \\cup f(T)$.

The next level proves this full equality using `ext`.

The proof pattern: extract the witness from the combined image,
determine which input set it came from (case split), then
redirect it to the corresponding output set. The witness and
its functional equation transfer unchanged ‚Äî only the membership
routing changes.
"

400‚Üí
   401‚ÜíWorld "FinsetImage"
   402‚ÜíLevel 6
   403‚Üí
   404‚ÜíTitle "Image Distributes Over Union: Full Equality"
   405‚Üí
   406‚ÜíIntroduction "
   407‚Üí# Proving the Full Equality
   408‚Üí
   409‚ÜíLevels 4 and 5 established both directions of the image-union
   410‚Üírelationship:
   411‚Üí- **Level 4**: $y \\in f(S) \\implies y \\in f(S \\cup T)$ (image grows)
   412‚Üí- **Level 5**: $y \\in f(S \\cup T) \\implies y \\in f(S) \\cup f(T)$
   413‚Üí  (elements route to one side)
   414‚Üí
   415‚ÜíTogether they give the fundamental identity:
   416‚Üí
   417‚Üí$$f(S \\cup T) = f(S) \\cup f(T)$$
   418‚Üí
   419‚ÜíTo prove a **finset equality**, use `ext`:
   420‚Üí```
   421‚Üíext x  -- reduces A = B to: x \\in A \\leftrightarrow x \\in B
   422‚Üí```
   423‚Üí
   424‚ÜíThis reduces the equality to a membership biconditional. Then use
   425‚Üí`constructor` to split into the two directions and apply the
   426‚Üípatterns from Levels 4 and 5.
   427‚Üí
   428‚Üí**Your task**: Prove the full equality using `ext` and both
   429‚Üímembership directions.
   430‚Üí"
   431‚Üí
   432‚Üí/-- Image distributes over union as a full equality. -/
   433‚ÜíStatement (f : ‚Ñï ‚Üí ‚Ñï) (s t : Finset ‚Ñï) :
   434‚Üí    (s ‚à™ t).image f = s.image f ‚à™ t.image f := by
   435‚Üí  Hint "Use `ext y` to reduce the finset equality to a membership
   436‚Üí  equivalence: `y ‚àà (s ‚à™ t).image f ‚Üî y ‚àà s.image f ‚à™ t.image f`."
   437‚Üí  ext y
   438‚Üí  Hint "The goal is an iff. Use `constructor` to split into both
   439‚Üí  directions."
   440‚Üí  constructor
   441‚Üí  -- Forward: y ‚àà (s ‚à™ t).image f ‚Üí y ‚àà s.image f ‚à™ t.image f
   442‚Üí  ¬∑ Hint "This is Level 5's argument: extract the witness, determine
   443‚Üí    which set it came from, and route to the correct side.
   444‚Üí    Start with `intro h` then `rw [Finset.mem_image] at h`."
   445‚Üí    intro h
   446‚Üí    rw [Finset.mem_image] at h
   447‚Üí    Hint "Use `obtain ‚ü®a, ha, hfa‚ü© := h` to extract the witness."
   448‚Üí    obtain ‚ü®a, ha, hfa‚ü© := h
   449‚Üí    Hint "Now `ha : a ‚àà s ‚à™ t`. Use `rw [Finset.mem_union] at ha`
   450‚Üí    to split into cases, then `rw [Finset.mem_union]` on the goal."
   451‚Üí    rw [Finset.mem_union] at ha
   452‚Üí    rw [Finset.mem_union]
   453‚Üí    Hint (hidden := true) "Try `cases ha with` then use `left`/`right`
   454‚Üí    with `rw [Finset.mem_image]; exact ‚ü®a, ..., hfa‚ü©`."
   455‚Üí    cases ha with
   456‚Üí    | inl hl =>
   457‚Üí      left
   458‚Üí      rw [Finset.mem_image]
   459‚Üí      exact ‚ü®a, hl, hfa‚ü©
   460‚Üí    | inr hr =>
   461‚Üí      right
   462‚Üí      rw [Finset.mem_image]
   463‚Üí      exact ‚ü®a, hr, hfa‚ü©
   464‚Üí  -- Backward: y ‚àà s.image f ‚à™ t.image f ‚Üí y ‚àà (s ‚à™ t).image f
   465‚Üí  ¬∑ Hint "This is the reverse: extract the witness from whichever
   466‚Üí    side of the union it came from, then embed it into `s ‚à™ t`.
   467‚Üí    Start with `intro h` then `rw [Finset.mem_union] at h`."
   468‚Üí    intro h
   469‚Üí    rw [Finset.mem_union] at h
   470‚Üí    Hint (hidden := true) "Try `cases h with` then extract the
   471‚Üí    witness and embed into the union."
   472‚Üí    cases h with
   473‚Üí    | inl hl =>
   474‚Üí      Hint "Extract the witness from `hl : y ‚àà s.image f`, then
   475‚Üí      provide it for `y ‚àà (s ‚à™ t).image f` with `a ‚àà s ‚à™ t`
   476‚Üí      via `rw [Finset.mem_union]` and `left`."
   477‚Üí      rw [Finset.mem_image] at hl
   478‚Üí      obtain ‚ü®a, ha, hfa‚ü© := hl
   479‚Üí      rw [Finset.mem_image]
   480‚Üí      use a
   481‚Üí      constructor
   482‚Üí      ¬∑ rw [Finset.mem_union]; left; exact ha
   483‚Üí      ¬∑ exact hfa
   484‚Üí    | inr hr =>
   485‚Üí      Hint "Same pattern for the right side."
   486‚Üí      rw [Finset.mem_image] at hr
   487‚Üí      obtain ‚ü®a, ha, hfa‚ü© := hr
   488‚Üí      rw [Finset.mem_image]
   489‚Üí      use a
   490‚Üí      constructor
   491‚Üí      ¬∑ rw [Finset.mem_union]; right; exact ha
   492‚Üí      ¬∑ exact hfa
   493‚Üí
   494‚ÜíConclusion "
   495‚ÜíYou proved the **full identity**: $f(S \\cup T) = f(S) \\cup f(T)$.
   496‚Üí
   497‚ÜíThe proof used `ext` to reduce equality to mutual membership,
   498‚Üíthen applied the forward direction (Level 5's argument) and the
   499‚Üíbackward direction (Level 4's pattern for both sides).
   500‚Üí
   501‚ÜíThis identity says **image is a homomorphism** from the
   502‚Üífinset union algebra to itself: it preserves the union operation.
   503‚Üí
   504‚ÜíCompare with intersection, where this breaks down ‚Äî you'll see
   505‚Üíwhy in Level 8.
   506‚Üí"

   514‚Üí
   515‚ÜíTitle "Image Respects Composition"
   516‚Üí
   517‚ÜíIntroduction "
   518‚Üí# The Algebraic Heart of Image
   519‚Üí
   520‚ÜíApplying $g$ then $f$ to a set gives the same result as applying
   521‚Üí$f \\circ g$ directly:
   522‚Üí
   523‚Üí$$(s.\\text{image}\\; g).\\text{image}\\; f \\;=\\; s.\\text{image}\\; (f \\circ g)$$
   524‚Üí
   525‚ÜíIn FinTuples you proved that function composition is associative.
   526‚ÜíNow you'll see that **image respects composition**: applying two
   527‚Üífunctions in sequence to a set is the same as applying their
   528‚Üícomposition.
   529‚Üí
   530‚ÜíThe proof uses `ext` and two layers of witness extraction:
   531‚Üí- **Forward**: extract a witness from the outer image, then extract
   532‚Üí  a deeper witness from the inner image
   533‚Üí- **Backward**: extract a witness from the composed image, then
   534‚Üí  build the two-layer membership
   535‚Üí
   536‚Üí**Your task**: Prove the image-composition identity.
   537‚Üí
   538‚Üí**Recall**: `Function.comp_apply` says `(f \\circ g) x = f (g x)`.
   539‚Üí"
   540‚Üí
   541‚Üí/-- Image respects function composition. -/
   542‚ÜíStatement (f g : ‚Ñï ‚Üí ‚Ñï) (s : Finset ‚Ñï) :
   543‚Üí    (s.image g).image f = s.image (f ‚àò g) := by
   544‚Üí  Hint "Use `ext y` to reduce the equality to element-wise membership."
   545‚Üí  ext y
   546‚Üí  Hint "Split the biconditional with `constructor`."
   547‚Üí  constructor
   548‚Üí  -- Forward: y ‚àà (s.image g).image f ‚Üí y ‚àà s.image (f ‚àò g)
   549‚Üí  ¬∑ Hint "Extract the witness from the outer image, then extract the
   550‚Üí    deeper witness from the inner image."
   551‚Üí    intro h
   552‚Üí    rw [Finset.mem_image] at h
   553‚Üí    obtain ‚ü®z, hz, hfz‚ü© := h
   554‚Üí    Hint "You have `z ‚àà s.image g`. Extract the original element
   555‚Üí    from `hz`."
   556‚Üí    rw [Finset.mem_image] at hz
   557‚Üí    obtain ‚ü®x, hx, hgx‚ü© := hz
   558‚Üí    Hint (hidden := true) "Now build `y ‚àà s.image (f ‚àò g)` with
   559‚Üí    witness `x`. Use `rw [Finset.mem_image]` on the goal,
   560‚Üí    then `exact ‚ü®x, hx, by rw [Function.comp_apply, hgx, hfz]‚ü©`."
   561‚Üí    rw [Finset.mem_image]
   562‚Üí    use x
   563‚Üí    constructor
   564‚Üí    ¬∑ exact hx
   565‚Üí    ¬∑ rw [Function.comp_apply, hgx, hfz]
   566‚Üí  -- Backward: y ‚àà s.image (f ‚àò g) ‚Üí y ‚àà (s.image g).image f
   567‚Üí  ¬∑ Hint "Extract the witness from the composed image, then build
   568‚Üí    the two-layer membership."
   569‚Üí    intro h
   570‚Üí    rw [Finset.mem_image] at h
   571‚Üí    obtain ‚ü®x, hx, hfgx‚ü© := h
   572‚Üí    Hint (hidden := true) "Provide `g x` as the witness for the outer image.
   573‚Üí    `rw [Finset.mem_image]`, `use g x`, then build `g x ‚àà s.image g`
   574‚Üí    with witness `x`, and show `f (g x) = y` from the composition."
   575‚Üí    rw [Finset.mem_image]
   576‚Üí    use g x
   577‚Üí    constructor
   578‚Üí    ¬∑ rw [Finset.mem_image]
   579‚Üí      exact ‚ü®x, hx, rfl‚ü©
   580‚Üí    ¬∑ rw [Function.comp_apply] at hfgx
   581‚Üí      exact hfgx
   582‚Üí
   583‚ÜíConclusion "
   584‚ÜíYou proved **image respects composition**:
   585‚Üí$(s.\\text{image}\\; g).\\text{image}\\; f = s.\\text{image}\\; (f \\circ g)$
   586‚Üí
   587‚ÜíThis is the fundamental algebraic property of image. Together with
   588‚ÜíLevel 6 (image distributes over union), you've shown that image
   589‚Üíis a **homomorphism**: it preserves both composition and union.
   590‚Üí
   591‚ÜíThe forward direction used **two-layer extraction**: pulling a
   592‚Üíwitness from the outer image, then a deeper witness from the inner.
   593‚ÜíThe backward direction used **two-layer construction**: building
   594‚Üímembership in the inner image first, then wrapping it in the outer.
   595‚Üí
   596‚ÜíIn Mathlib, this identity is `Finset.image_image`.
   597‚Üí"
   598‚Üí
   599‚Üí/-- `Finset.image_image` states that `(s.image f).image g = s.image (g ‚àò f)`:
   600‚Üíimage respects function composition. -/
   601‚ÜíTheoremDoc Finset.image_image as "Finset.image_image" in "Finset"

   608‚ÜíLevel 8
   609‚Üí
   610‚ÜíTitle "Image of a Singleton"
   611‚Üí
   612‚ÜíIntroduction "
   613‚Üí# Boundary Cases: What Happens at the Extremes?
   614‚Üí
   615‚ÜíA key boundary case for image:
   616‚Üí
   617‚Üí**Singleton**: The image of a singleton $\\{a\\}$ under $f$ is
   618‚Üí$\\{f(a)\\}$ ‚Äî there's exactly one element to map, producing
   619‚Üíexactly one output. In Lean: `Finset.image_singleton`.
   620‚Üí
   621‚ÜíThis is proved in Mathlib, but proving it manually teaches
   622‚Üían important extraction pattern.
   623‚Üí
   624‚Üí**Your task**: Given that `x` is in the image of `{a}` under `f`,
   625‚Üíprove that `x = f a`. In other words: the only element in the
   626‚Üíimage of a singleton is the function applied to that element.
   627‚Üí
   628‚ÜíStrategy: extract the witness from `h`, observe it must equal `a`
   629‚Üí(the only element), then derive `x = f a`.
   630‚Üí"
   631‚Üí
   632‚Üí/-- Every element in the image of a singleton equals f applied to that element. -/
   633‚ÜíStatement (f : ‚Ñï ‚Üí ‚Ñï) (a x : ‚Ñï)
   634‚Üí    (h : x ‚àà ({a} : Finset ‚Ñï).image f) : x = f a := by
   635‚Üí  Hint "Extract the witness from `h`. Use `rw [Finset.mem_image] at h`."
   636‚Üí  rw [Finset.mem_image] at h
   637‚Üí  Hint "Use `obtain ‚ü®b, hb, hfb‚ü© := h` to get the witness `b`,
   638‚Üí  its membership `hb : b ‚àà singleton a`, and `hfb : f b = x`."
   639‚Üí  obtain ‚ü®b, hb, hfb‚ü© := h
   640‚Üí  Hint "Now `hb : b ‚àà singleton a`. Use `rw [Finset.mem_singleton] at hb`
   641‚Üí  to get `hb : b = a`."
   642‚Üí  rw [Finset.mem_singleton] at hb
   643‚Üí  Hint "You have `hb : b = a` and `hfb : f b = x`. Rewrite `b` to `a`
   644‚Üí  in `hfb` using `rw [hb] at hfb`, then use the result."
   645‚Üí  Hint (hidden := true) "After `rw [hb] at hfb`, you have `hfb : f a = x`.
   646‚Üí  Then `exact hfb.symm` closes the goal."
   647‚Üí  rw [hb] at hfb
   648‚Üí  exact hfb.symm
   649‚Üí
   650‚ÜíConclusion "
   651‚ÜíThe singleton extraction pattern:
   652‚Üí1. Extract the witness: `obtain ‚ü®b, hb, hfb‚ü© := h`
   653‚Üí2. Observe the witness is determined: `rw [Finset.mem_singleton] at hb`
   654‚Üí3. Substitute and conclude
   655‚Üí
   656‚ÜíThis confirms: $f(\\{a\\}) = \\{f(a)\\}$. The image of a singleton
   657‚Üíis a singleton.
   658‚Üí
   659‚ÜíThis boundary case anchors the general behavior: image can only
   660‚Üíshrink cardinality (by collapsing), never expand it.
   661‚Üí"

   668‚ÜíLevel 9
   669‚Üí
   670‚ÜíTitle "Image of Intersection: The Easy Direction"
   671‚Üí
   672‚ÜíIntroduction "
   673‚Üí# Does Image Distribute Over Intersection?
   674‚Üí
   675‚ÜíYou proved in Level 5 that image distributes over union:
   676‚Üí$f(S \\cup T) = f(S) \\cup f(T)$. A natural question: does the
   677‚Üísame hold for intersection?
   678‚Üí
   679‚Üí$$f(S \\cap T) \\stackrel{?}{=} f(S) \\cap f(T)$$
   680‚Üí
   681‚ÜíThe answer is **no** in general ‚Äî but one direction always holds.
   682‚Üí
   683‚ÜíIf $y \\in f(S \\cap T)$, then the witness $x$ is in BOTH $S$ and
   684‚Üí$T$, so $f(x) \\in f(S)$ AND $f(x) \\in f(T)$. This gives:
   685‚Üí
   686‚Üí$$f(S \\cap T) \\subseteq f(S) \\cap f(T)$$
   687‚Üí
   688‚ÜíThe reverse can fail: $f(S)$ and $f(T)$ might share an output
   689‚Üívalue even when $S$ and $T$ don't share the input that produced it.
   690‚ÜíFor example, if $f(2) = f(3) = 0$, $S = \\{2\\}$, $T = \\{3\\}$,
   691‚Üíthen $0 \\in f(S) \\cap f(T)$ but $S \\cap T = \\emptyset$.
   692‚Üí
   693‚Üí**Your task**: Prove the easy direction.
   694‚Üí"
   695‚Üí
   696‚Üí/-- The image of an intersection is contained in the intersection of images. -/
   697‚ÜíStatement (f : ‚Ñï ‚Üí ‚Ñï) (s t : Finset ‚Ñï) :
   698‚Üí    (s ‚à© t).image f ‚äÜ s.image f ‚à© t.image f := by
   699‚Üí  Hint "A subset goal unfolds to `‚àÄ y, y ‚àà lhs ‚Üí y ‚àà rhs`.
   700‚Üí  Use `intro y hy` to introduce an element and its membership proof."
   701‚Üí  intro y hy
   702‚Üí  Hint "Now `hy : y ‚àà (s ‚à© t).image f`. Extract the witness with
   703‚Üí  `rw [Finset.mem_image] at hy`, then `obtain`."
   704‚Üí  rw [Finset.mem_image] at hy
   705‚Üí  Hint "Use `obtain ‚ü®x, hx, hfx‚ü© := hy` to extract the witness `x`,
   706‚Üí  its membership `hx : x ‚àà s ‚à© t`, and `hfx : f x = y`."
   707‚Üí  obtain ‚ü®x, hx, hfx‚ü© := hy
   708‚Üí  Hint "Now `hx : x ‚àà s ‚à© t`. Use `rw [Finset.mem_inter] at hx`
   709‚Üí  to split into `x ‚àà s ‚àß x ‚àà t`."
   710‚Üí  rw [Finset.mem_inter] at hx
   711‚Üí  Hint "Use `obtain ‚ü®hxs, hxt‚ü© := hx` to get `hxs : x ‚àà s` and
   712‚Üí  `hxt : x ‚àà t` separately."
   713‚Üí  obtain ‚ü®hxs, hxt‚ü© := hx
   714‚Üí  Hint "The goal is `y ‚àà s.image f ‚à© t.image f`. Use
   715‚Üí  `rw [Finset.mem_inter]` to convert to a conjunction, then
   716‚Üí  `constructor` to split."
   717‚Üí  rw [Finset.mem_inter]
   718‚Üí  constructor
   719‚Üí  ¬∑ Hint "Prove `y ‚àà s.image f` using `x ‚àà s` as the witness.
   720‚Üí    Try `rw [Finset.mem_image]` then `exact ‚ü®x, hxs, hfx‚ü©`."
   721‚Üí    Hint (hidden := true) "Try `rw [Finset.mem_image]; exact ‚ü®x, hxs, hfx‚ü©`."
   722‚Üí    rw [Finset.mem_image]
   723‚Üí    exact ‚ü®x, hxs, hfx‚ü©
   724‚Üí  ¬∑ Hint "Prove `y ‚àà t.image f` using `x ‚àà t` as the witness.
   725‚Üí    Try `rw [Finset.mem_image]` then `exact ‚ü®x, hxt, hfx‚ü©`."
   726‚Üí    Hint (hidden := true) "Try `rw [Finset.mem_image]; exact ‚ü®x, hxt, hfx‚ü©`."
   727‚Üí    rw [Finset.mem_image]
   728‚Üí    exact ‚ü®x, hxt, hfx‚ü©
   729‚Üí
   730‚ÜíConclusion "
   731‚ÜíYou proved $f(S \\cap T) \\subseteq f(S) \\cap f(T)$.
   732‚Üí
   733‚ÜíThe proof used the same extract-and-reuse pattern from Level 4:
   734‚Üí1. Extract the witness from the hypothesis
   735‚Üí2. Observe it satisfies BOTH membership conditions
   736‚Üí3. Provide it as the witness for BOTH sides of the intersection
   737‚Üí
   738‚ÜíBut unlike union, the **reverse** direction fails in general.
   739‚ÜíThe counterexample: $f(2) = f(3) = 0$, $S = \\{2\\}$,
   740‚Üí$T = \\{3\\}$. Then $0 \\in f(S) \\cap f(T)$ but
   741‚Üí$S \\cap T = \\emptyset$, so $0 \\notin f(S \\cap T)$.
   742‚Üí
   743‚ÜíWhat goes wrong? When you extract witnesses from both sides
   744‚Üí($a \\in S$ with $f(a) = y$ and $b \\in T$ with $f(b) = y$),
   745‚Üíyou get **two different witnesses** and can't conclude $a = b$
   746‚Üí‚Äî unless $f$ is **injective**. You'll prove this repair later
   747‚Üíin this world.
   748‚Üí
   749‚Üí**Why union works but intersection doesn't**: Image is inherently
   750‚Üí*surjective onto its own elements* ‚Äî every $y \\in f(S)$ has a
   751‚Üípreimage in $S$ by definition. Surjectivity gives the 'easy
   752‚Üídirection' for free in both the union and intersection cases. The
   753‚Üíquestion is always the 'hard direction': does every element of
   754‚Üí$f(S) \\cap f(T)$ come from $S \\cap T$? For union, yes (always).
   755‚ÜíFor intersection, only if $f$ is injective ‚Äî because without
   756‚Üíinjectivity, different preimages can produce the same output.
   757‚Üí"
   758‚Üí

   760‚ÜíDisabledTheorem Finset.mem_insert_self Finset.mem_insert_of_mem Finset.mem_image_of_mem Finset.image_subset_image Finset.image_mono Finset.image_inter_subset Finset.image_inter Finset.image_inter_of_injOn Finset.mem_inter_of_mem Finset.mem_of_mem_inter_left Finset.mem_of_mem_inter_right Finset.inter_subset_left Finset.inter_subset_right inf_le_left inf_le_right
   761‚Üíimport Game.Metadata
   762‚Üí
   763‚ÜíWorld "FinsetImage"
   764‚ÜíLevel 10
   765‚Üí
   766‚ÜíTitle "Why Injectivity Matters: A Concrete Failure"
   767‚Üí
   768‚ÜíIntroduction "
   769‚Üí# Seeing the Failure Without Injectivity
   770‚Üí
   771‚ÜíIn Level 9, you proved that $f(S \\cap T) \\subseteq f(S) \\cap f(T)$
   772‚Üíalways holds. The reverse direction can fail when $f$ is not injective.
   773‚ÜíYou saw the counterexample in the conclusion ‚Äî now you'll **prove** it.
   774‚Üí
   775‚ÜíTake $f(x) = 0$ (the constant function), $S = \\{1\\}$, $T = \\{2\\}$.
   776‚Üí
   777‚Üí- $S \\cap T = \\emptyset$, so $f(S \\cap T) = f(\\emptyset) = \\emptyset$
   778‚Üí- But $f(S) = \\{0\\}$ and $f(T) = \\{0\\}$, so $f(S) \\cap f(T) = \\{0\\}$
   779‚Üí
   780‚ÜíThe intersection of the images is non-empty, but the image of the
   781‚Üíintersection is empty. The function $f$ creates a 'phantom' intersection
   782‚Üíelement: $0$ appears in both images but comes from different sources
   783‚Üí($1 \\in S$ and $2 \\in T$), not from a shared source in $S \\cap T$.
   784‚Üí
   785‚Üí**Your task**: Prove that $0 \\in f(S) \\cap f(T)$ but $0 \\notin f(S \\cap T)$.
   786‚ÜíThis witnesses the strict containment $f(S \\cap T) \\subsetneq f(S) \\cap f(T)$.
   787‚Üí"
   788‚Üí
   789‚Üí/-- A concrete witness that image of intersection ‚ää intersection of images
   790‚Üíwhen f is not injective. -/
   791‚ÜíStatement : (0 : ‚Ñï) ‚àà ({1} : Finset ‚Ñï).image (fun _ => (0 : ‚Ñï)) ‚à©
   792‚Üí              ({2} : Finset ‚Ñï).image (fun _ => (0 : ‚Ñï)) ‚àß
   793‚Üí            (0 : ‚Ñï) ‚àâ (({1} : Finset ‚Ñï) ‚à© {2}).image (fun _ => (0 : ‚Ñï)) := by
   794‚Üí  Hint "Split the conjunction with `constructor`."
   795‚Üí  constructor
   796‚Üí  -- Part 1: 0 ‚àà f({1}) ‚à© f({2})
   797‚Üí  ¬∑ Hint "The goal is `0 ‚àà image(...) ‚à© image(...)`. Rewrite with
   798‚Üí    `Finset.mem_inter` to split into two membership goals."
   799‚Üí    rw [Finset.mem_inter]

file.content:
World "FinsetImage"
Level 6

Title "Image Distributes Over Union: Full Equality"

Introduction "
# Proving the Full Equality

Levels 4 and 5 established both directions of the image-union
relationship:
- **Level 4**: $y \\in f(S) \\implies y \\in f(S \\cup T)$ (image grows)
- **Level 5**: $y \\in f(S \\cup T) \\implies y \\in f(S) \\cup f(T)$
  (elements route to one side)

Together they give the fundamental identity:

$$f(S \\cup T) = f(S) \\cup f(T)$$

To prove a **finset equality**, use `ext`:
```
ext x  -- reduces A = B to: x \\in A \\leftrightarrow x \\in B
```

This reduces the equality to a membership biconditional. Then use
`constructor` to split into the two directions and apply the
patterns from Levels 4 and 5.

**Your task**: Prove the full equality using `ext` and both
membership directions.
"

/-- Image distributes over union as a full equality. -/
Statement (f : ‚Ñï ‚Üí ‚Ñï) (s t : Finset ‚Ñï) :
    (s ‚à™ t).image f = s.image f ‚à™ t.image f := by
  Hint "Use `ext y` to reduce the finset equality to a membership
  equivalence: `y ‚àà (s ‚à™ t).image f ‚Üî y ‚àà s.image f ‚à™ t.image f`."
  ext y
  Hint "The goal is an iff. Use `constructor` to split into both
  directions."
  constructor
  -- Forward: y ‚àà (s ‚à™ t).image f ‚Üí y ‚àà s.image f ‚à™ t.image f
  ¬∑ Hint "This is Level 5's argument: extract the witness, determine
    which set it came from, and route to the correct side.
    Start with `intro h` then `rw [Finset.mem_image] at h`."
    intro h
    rw [Finset.mem_image] at h
    Hint "Use `obtain ‚ü®a, ha, hfa‚ü© := h` to extract the witness."
    obtain ‚ü®a, ha, hfa‚ü© := h
    Hint "Now `ha : a ‚àà s ‚à™ t`. Use `rw [Finset.mem_union] at ha`
    to split into cases, then `rw [Finset.mem_union]` on the goal."
    rw [Finset.mem_union] at ha
    rw [Finset.mem_union]
    Hint (hidden := true) "Try `cases ha with` then use `left`/`right`
    with `rw [Finset.mem_image]; exact ‚ü®a, ..., hfa‚ü©`."
    cases ha with
    | inl hl =>
      left
      rw [Finset.mem_image]
      exact ‚ü®a, hl, hfa‚ü©
    | inr hr =>
      right
      rw [Finset.mem_image]
      exact ‚ü®a, hr, hfa‚ü©
  -- Backward: y ‚àà s.image f ‚à™ t.image f ‚Üí y ‚àà (s ‚à™ t).image f
  ¬∑ Hint "This is the reverse: extract the witness from whichever
    side of the union it came from, then embed it into `s ‚à™ t`.
    Start with `intro h` then `rw [Finset.mem_union] at h`."
    intro h
    rw [Finset.mem_union] at h
    Hint (hidden := true) "Try `cases h with` then extract the
    witness and embed into the union."
    cases h with
    | inl hl =>
      Hint "Extract the witness from `hl : y ‚àà s.image f`, then
      provide it for `y ‚àà (s ‚à™ t).image f` with `a ‚àà s ‚à™ t`
      via `rw [Finset.mem_union]` and `left`."
      rw [Finset.mem_image] at hl
      obtain ‚ü®a, ha, hfa‚ü© := hl
      rw [Finset.mem_image]
      use a
      constructor
      ¬∑ rw [Finset.mem_union]; left; exact ha
      ¬∑ exact hfa
    | inr hr =>
      Hint "Same pattern for the right side."
      rw [Finset.mem_image] at hr
      obtain ‚ü®a, ha, hfa‚ü© := hr
      rw [Finset.mem_image]
      use a
      constructor
      ¬∑ rw [Finset.mem_union]; right; exact ha
      ¬∑ exact hfa

Conclusion "
You proved the **full identity**: $f(S \\cup T) = f(S) \\cup f(T)$.

The proof used `ext` to reduce equality to mutual membership,
then applied the forward direction (Level 5's argument) and the
backward direction (Level 4's pattern for both sides).

This identity says **image is a homomorphism** from the
finset union algebra to itself: it preserves the union operation.

Compare with intersection, where this breaks down ‚Äî you'll see
why in Level 8.
"

World "FinsetImage"
Level 7

Title "Image Respects Composition"

Introduction "
# The Algebraic Heart of Image

Applying $g$ then $f$ to a set gives the same result as applying
$f \\circ g$ directly:

$$(s.\\text{image}\\; g).\\text{image}\\; f \\;=\\; s.\\text{image}\\; (f \\circ g)$$

In FinTuples you proved that function composition is associative.
Now you'll see that **image respects composition**: applying two
functions in sequence to a set is the same as applying their
composition.

The proof uses `ext` and two layers of witness extraction:
- **Forward**: extract a witness from the outer image, then extract
  a deeper witness from the inner image
- **Backward**: extract a witness from the composed image, then
  build the two-layer membership

**Your task**: Prove the image-composition identity.

**Recall**: `Function.comp_apply` says `(f \\circ g) x = f (g x)`.
"

/-- Image respects function composition. -/
Statement (f g : ‚Ñï ‚Üí ‚Ñï) (s : Finset ‚Ñï) :
    (s.image g).image f = s.image (f ‚àò g) := by
  Hint "Use `ext y` to reduce the equality to element-wise membership."
  ext y
  Hint "Split the biconditional with `constructor`."
  constructor
  -- Forward: y ‚àà (s.image g).image f ‚Üí y ‚àà s.image (f ‚àò g)
  ¬∑ Hint "Extract the witness from the outer image, then extract the
    deeper witness from the inner image."
    intro h
    rw [Finset.mem_image] at h
    obtain ‚ü®z, hz, hfz‚ü© := h
    Hint "You have `z ‚àà s.image g`. Extract the original element
    from `hz`."
    rw [Finset.mem_image] at hz
    obtain ‚ü®x, hx, hgx‚ü© := hz
    Hint (hidden := true) "Now build `y ‚àà s.image (f ‚àò g)` with
    witness `x`. Use `rw [Finset.mem_image]` on the goal,
    then `exact ‚ü®x, hx, by rw [Function.comp_apply, hgx, hfz]‚ü©`."
    rw [Finset.mem_image]
    use x
    constructor
    ¬∑ exact hx
    ¬∑ rw [Function.comp_apply, hgx, hfz]
  -- Backward: y ‚àà s.image (f ‚àò g) ‚Üí y ‚àà (s.image g).image f
  ¬∑ Hint "Extract the witness from the composed image, then build
    the two-layer membership."
    intro h
    rw [Finset.mem_image] at h
    obtain ‚ü®x, hx, hfgx‚ü© := h
    Hint (hidden := true) "Provide `g x` as the witness for the outer image.
    `rw [Finset.mem_image]`, `use g x`, then build `g x ‚àà s.image g`
    with witness `x`, and show `f (g x) = y` from the composition."
    rw [Finset.mem_image]
    use g x
    constructor
    ¬∑ rw [Finset.mem_image]
      exact ‚ü®x, hx, rfl‚ü©
    ¬∑ rw [Function.comp_apply] at hfgx
      exact hfgx

Conclusion "
You proved **image respects composition**:
$(s.\\text{image}\\; g).\\text{image}\\; f = s.\\text{image}\\; (f \\circ g)$

This is the fundamental algebraic property of image. Together with
Level 6 (image distributes over union), you've shown that image
is a **homomorphism**: it preserves both composition and union.

The forward direction used **two-layer extraction**: pulling a
witness from the outer image, then a deeper witness from the inner.
The backward direction used **two-layer construction**: building
membership in the inner image first, then wrapping it in the outer.

In Mathlib, this identity is `Finset.image_image`.
"

/-- `Finset.image_image` states that `(s.image f).image g = s.image (g ‚àò f)`:
image respects function composition. -/
TheoremDoc Finset.image_image as "Finset.image_image" in "Finset"

World "FinsetImage"
Level 8

Title "Image of a Singleton"

Introduction "
# Boundary Cases: What Happens at the Extremes?

A key boundary case for image:

**Singleton**: The image of a singleton $\\{a\\}$ under $f$ is
$\\{f(a)\\}$ ‚Äî there's exactly one element to map, producing
exactly one output. In Lean: `Finset.image_singleton`.

This is proved in Mathlib, but proving it manually teaches
an important extraction pattern.

**Your task**: Given that `x` is in the image of `{a}` under `f`,
prove that `x = f a`. In other words: the only element in the
image of a singleton is the function applied to that element.

Strategy: extract the witness from `h`, observe it must equal `a`
(the only element), then derive `x = f a`.
"

/-- Every element in the image of a singleton equals f applied to that element. -/
Statement (f : ‚Ñï ‚Üí ‚Ñï) (a x : ‚Ñï)
    (h : x ‚àà ({a} : Finset ‚Ñï).image f) : x = f a := by
  Hint "Extract the witness from `h`. Use `rw [Finset.mem_image] at h`."
  rw [Finset.mem_image] at h
  Hint "Use `obtain ‚ü®b, hb, hfb‚ü© := h` to get the witness `b`,
  its membership `hb : b ‚àà singleton a`, and `hfb : f b = x`."
  obtain ‚ü®b, hb, hfb‚ü© := h
  Hint "Now `hb : b ‚àà singleton a`. Use `rw [Finset.mem_singleton] at hb`
  to get `hb : b = a`."
  rw [Finset.mem_singleton] at hb
  Hint "You have `hb : b = a` and `hfb : f b = x`. Rewrite `b` to `a`
  in `hfb` using `rw [hb] at hfb`, then use the result."
  Hint (hidden := true) "After `rw [hb] at hfb`, you have `hfb : f a = x`.
  Then `exact hfb.symm` closes the goal."
  rw [hb] at hfb
  exact hfb.symm

Conclusion "
The singleton extraction pattern:
1. Extract the witness: `obtain ‚ü®b, hb, hfb‚ü© := h`
2. Observe the witness is determined: `rw [Finset.mem_singleton] at hb`
3. Substitute and conclude

This confirms: $f(\\{a\\}) = \\{f(a)\\}$. The image of a singleton
is a singleton.

This boundary case anchors the general behavior: image can only
shrink cardinality (by collapsing), never expand it.
"

World "FinsetImage"
Level 9

Title "Image of Intersection: The Easy Direction"

Introduction "
# Does Image Distribute Over Intersection?

You proved in Level 5 that image distributes over union:
$f(S \\cup T) = f(S) \\cup f(T)$. A natural question: does the
same hold for intersection?

$$f(S \\cap T) \\stackrel{?}{=} f(S) \\cap f(T)$$

The answer is **no** in general ‚Äî but one direction always holds.

If $y \\in f(S \\cap T)$, then the witness $x$ is in BOTH $S$ and
$T$, so $f(x) \\in f(S)$ AND $f(x) \\in f(T)$. This gives:

$$f(S \\cap T) \\subseteq f(S) \\cap f(T)$$

The reverse can fail: $f(S)$ and $f(T)$ might share an output
value even when $S$ and $T$ don't share the input that produced it.
For example, if $f(2) = f(3) = 0$, $S = \\{2\\}$, $T = \\{3\\}$,
then $0 \\in f(S) \\cap f(T)$ but $S \\cap T = \\emptyset$.

**Your task**: Prove the easy direction.
"

/-- The image of an intersection is contained in the intersection of images. -/
Statement (f : ‚Ñï ‚Üí ‚Ñï) (s t : Finset ‚Ñï) :
    (s ‚à© t).image f ‚äÜ s.image f ‚à© t.image f := by
  Hint "A subset goal unfolds to `‚àÄ y, y ‚àà lhs ‚Üí y ‚àà rhs`.
  Use `intro y hy` to introduce an element and its membership proof."
  intro y hy
  Hint "Now `hy : y ‚àà (s ‚à© t).image f`. Extract the witness with
  `rw [Finset.mem_image] at hy`, then `obtain`."
  rw [Finset.mem_image] at hy
  Hint "Use `obtain ‚ü®x, hx, hfx‚ü© := hy` to extract the witness `x`,
  its membership `hx : x ‚àà s ‚à© t`, and `hfx : f x = y`."
  obtain ‚ü®x, hx, hfx‚ü© := hy
  Hint "Now `hx : x ‚àà s ‚à© t`. Use `rw [Finset.mem_inter] at hx`
  to split into `x ‚àà s ‚àß x ‚àà t`."
  rw [Finset.mem_inter] at hx
  Hint "Use `obtain ‚ü®hxs, hxt‚ü© := hx` to get `hxs : x ‚àà s` and
  `hxt : x ‚àà t` separately."
  obtain ‚ü®hxs, hxt‚ü© := hx
  Hint "The goal is `y ‚àà s.image f ‚à© t.image f`. Use
  `rw [Finset.mem_inter]` to convert to a conjunction, then
  `constructor` to split."
  rw [Finset.mem_inter]
  constructor
  ¬∑ Hint "Prove `y ‚àà s.image f` using `x ‚àà s` as the witness.
    Try `rw [Finset.mem_image]` then `exact ‚ü®x, hxs, hfx‚ü©`."
    Hint (hidden := true) "Try `rw [Finset.mem_image]; exact ‚ü®x, hxs, hfx‚ü©`."
    rw [Finset.mem_image]
    exact ‚ü®x, hxs, hfx‚ü©
  ¬∑ Hint "Prove `y ‚àà t.image f` using `x ‚àà t` as the witness.
    Try `rw [Finset.mem_image]` then `exact ‚ü®x, hxt, hfx‚ü©`."
    Hint (hidden := true) "Try `rw [Finset.mem_image]; exact ‚ü®x, hxt, hfx‚ü©`."
    rw [Finset.mem_image]
    exact ‚ü®x, hxt, hfx‚ü©

Conclusion "
You proved $f(S \\cap T) \\subseteq f(S) \\cap f(T)$.

The proof used the same extract-and-reuse pattern from Level 4:
1. Extract the witness from the hypothesis
2. Observe it satisfies BOTH membership conditions
3. Provide it as the witness for BOTH sides of the intersection

But unlike union, the **reverse** direction fails in general.
The counterexample: $f(2) = f(3) = 0$, $S = \\{2\\}$,
$T = \\{3\\}$. Then $0 \\in f(S) \\cap f(T)$ but
$S \\cap T = \\emptyset$, so $0 \\notin f(S \\cap T)$.

What goes wrong? When you extract witnesses from both sides
($a \\in S$ with $f(a) = y$ and $b \\in T$ with $f(b) = y$),
you get **two different witnesses** and can't conclude $a = b$
‚Äî unless $f$ is **injective**. You'll prove this repair later
in this world.

**Why union works but intersection doesn't**: Image is inherently
*surjective onto its own elements* ‚Äî every $y \\in f(S)$ has a
preimage in $S$ by definition. Surjectivity gives the 'easy
direction' for free in both the union and intersection cases. The
question is always the 'hard direction': does every element of
$f(S) \\cap f(T)$ come from $S \\cap T$? For union, yes (always).
For intersection, only if $f$ is injective ‚Äî because without
injectivity, different preimages can produce the same output.
"

World "FinsetImage"
Level 10

Title "Why Injectivity Matters: A Concrete Failure"

Introduction "
# Seeing the Failure Without Injectivity

In Level 9, you proved that $f(S \\cap T) \\subseteq f(S) \\cap f(T)$
always holds. The reverse direction can fail when $f$ is not injective.
You saw the counterexample in the conclusion ‚Äî now you'll **prove** it.

Take $f(x) = 0$ (the constant function), $S = \\{1\\}$, $T = \\{2\\}$.

- $S \\cap T = \\emptyset$, so $f(S \\cap T) = f(\\emptyset) = \\emptyset$
- But $f(S) = \\{0\\}$ and $f(T) = \\{0\\}$, so $f(S) \\cap f(T) = \\{0\\}$

The intersection of the images is non-empty, but the image of the
intersection is empty. The function $f$ creates a 'phantom' intersection
element: $0$ appears in both images but comes from different sources
($1 \\in S$ and $2 \\in T$), not from a shared source in $S \\cap T$.

**Your task**: Prove that $0 \\in f(S) \\cap f(T)$ but $0 \\notin f(S \\cap T)$.
This witnesses the strict containment $f(S \\cap T) \\subsetneq f(S) \\cap f(T)$.
"

/-- A concrete witness that image of intersection ‚ää intersection of images
when f is not injective. -/
Statement : (0 : ‚Ñï) ‚àà ({1} : Finset ‚Ñï).image (fun _ => (0 : ‚Ñï)) ‚à©
              ({2} : Finset ‚Ñï).image (fun _ => (0 : ‚Ñï)) ‚àß
            (0 : ‚Ñï) ‚àâ (({1} : Finset ‚Ñï) ‚à© {2}).image (fun _ => (0 : ‚Ñï)) := by
  Hint "Split the conjunction with `constructor`."
  constructor
  -- Part 1: 0 ‚àà f({1}) ‚à© f({2})
  ¬∑ Hint "The goal is `0 ‚àà image(...) ‚à© image(...)`. Rewrite with
    `Finset.mem_inter` to split into two membership goals."
    rw [Finset.mem_inter]

content.content:
300‚Üí"
   301‚Üí
   302‚Üí/-- `Finset.card_eq_zero` states that `s.card = 0 ‚Üî s = ‚àÖ`.
   303‚Üí
   304‚ÜíA finset has zero elements if and only if it is the empty set.
   305‚Üí
   306‚Üí## Syntax
   307‚Üí```
   308‚Üíexact Finset.card_eq_zero.mp hs  -- from s.card = 0 to s = ‚àÖ
   309‚Üírw [‚Üê Finset.card_eq_zero]      -- convert goal s = ‚àÖ to s.card = 0
   310‚Üí```
   311‚Üí
   312‚Üí## When to use it
   313‚ÜíWhen you know `s.card = 0` and need `s = ‚àÖ`, or vice versa.
   314‚Üí-/
   315‚ÜíTheoremDoc Finset.card_eq_zero as "Finset.card_eq_zero" in "Card"
   316‚Üí
   317‚ÜíTheoremTab "Card"
   318‚ÜíNewTheorem Finset.card_eq_zero
   319‚Üí
   320‚ÜíDisabledTactic trivial ¬´decide¬ª native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num by_cases tauto linarith
   321‚Üíimport Game.Metadata
   322‚Üí
   323‚ÜíWorld "Cardinality"
   324‚ÜíLevel 6
   325‚Üí
   326‚ÜíTitle "Insert Adds One"
   327‚Üí
   328‚ÜíIntroduction "
   329‚Üí# Inserting a New Element
   330‚Üí
   331‚ÜíIf `a` is **not** already in `s`, then inserting `a` increases the
   332‚Üícardinality by exactly one:
   333‚Üí
   334‚Üí$a \\notin s \\implies |\\text{insert}(a, s)| = |s| + 1$
   335‚Üí
   336‚ÜíIn Lean:
   337‚Üí```
   338‚ÜíFinset.card_insert_of_notMem : a ‚àâ s ‚Üí (insert a s).card = s.card + 1
   339‚Üí```
   340‚Üí
   341‚ÜíThe hypothesis `a ‚àâ s` is essential. If `a` were already in `s`, then
   342‚Üí`insert a s = s` (by the idempotency you saw in FinsetBasics), and
   343‚Üíthe cardinality wouldn't change. The theorem requires you to *prove*
   344‚Üíthe element is new before it promises the count goes up.
   345‚Üí
   346‚Üí**Your task**: Given that `a ‚àâ s`, prove that inserting `a` increases
   347‚Üíthe cardinality by 1.
   348‚Üí"
   349‚Üí
   350‚Üí/-- Inserting a new element increases cardinality by 1. -/
   351‚ÜíStatement (s : Finset ‚Ñï) (a : ‚Ñï) (h : a ‚àâ s) :
   352‚Üí    (insert a s).card = s.card + 1 := by
   353‚Üí  Hint "You have `h : a ‚àâ s` and need to show `(insert a s).card = s.card + 1`.
   354‚Üí  This is exactly `Finset.card_insert_of_notMem h`."
   355‚Üí  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `exact Finset.card_insert_of_notMem h`."
   356‚Üí  exact Finset.card_insert_of_notMem h
   357‚Üí
   358‚ÜíConclusion "
   359‚Üí`Finset.card_insert_of_notMem` is the inductive step for cardinality.
   360‚ÜíCombined with the base cases (`card_empty`, `card_singleton`), it lets
   361‚Üíyou compute the cardinality of any finset built by iterated insertion:
   362‚Üí
   363‚Üí$$|\\emptyset| = 0, \\quad |\\{a\\}| = 1, \\quad |\\text{insert}(a, s)| = |s| + 1 \\text{ (if } a \\notin s\\text{)}$$
   364‚Üí
   365‚ÜíThe non-membership hypothesis is the critical ingredient ‚Äî without it,
   366‚Üíthe lemma doesn't apply. The next level puts this to work: computing
   367‚Üíthe cardinality of a concrete finset by chaining `card_insert` steps.
   368‚Üí"
   369‚Üí
   370‚Üí/-- `Finset.card_insert_of_notMem h` states that when `h : a ‚àâ s`,
   371‚Üí`(insert a s).card = s.card + 1`.
   372‚Üí
   373‚Üí## Syntax
   374‚Üí```
   375‚Üíexact Finset.card_insert_of_notMem h
   376‚Üírw [Finset.card_insert_of_notMem h]
   377‚Üí```
   378‚Üí
   379‚Üí## When to use it
   380‚ÜíWhen the goal involves the cardinality of `insert a s` and you know
   381‚Üí(or can prove) that `a ‚àâ s`.
   382‚Üí
   383‚Üí## Important
   384‚ÜíThe hypothesis `a ‚àâ s` is required. If `a ‚àà s`, then `insert a s = s`
   385‚Üíand the cardinality does not change.
   386‚Üí
   387‚Üí## Example
   388‚Üí```
   389‚Üí-- h : 5 ‚àâ s
   390‚Üí-- Goal: (insert 5 s).card = s.card + 1
   391‚Üíexact Finset.card_insert_of_notMem h
   392‚Üí```
   393‚Üí-/

   396‚ÜíTheoremTab "Card"
   397‚ÜíNewTheorem Finset.card_insert_of_notMem
   398‚Üí
   399‚ÜíDisabledTactic trivial ¬´decide¬ª native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num by_cases tauto linarith
   400‚Üíimport Game.Metadata
   401‚Üí
   402‚ÜíWorld "Cardinality"
   403‚ÜíLevel 7
   404‚Üí
   405‚ÜíTitle "Insert Doesn't Always Add One"
   406‚Üí
   407‚ÜíIntroduction "
   408‚Üí# When Insert is a No-Op
   409‚Üí
   410‚ÜíIn Level 6, you learned `card_insert_of_notMem`: inserting a NEW
   411‚Üíelement adds 1 to the cardinality. But what if the element is
   412‚ÜíALREADY in the set?
   413‚Üí
   414‚ÜíYou proved in FinsetBasics that `Finset.insert_eq_of_mem`:
   415‚Üíif `a \\in s`, then `insert a s = s` ‚Äî insert is a no-op.
   416‚Üí
   417‚ÜíThe cardinality consequence: if `a \\in s`, then
   418‚Üí`(insert a s).card = s.card` ‚Äî the count doesn't change.
   419‚Üí
   420‚ÜíThis addresses a natural misconception: `card(insert a s) = card s + 1`
   421‚Üíis NOT always true. It's only true when `a \\notin s`. When `a \\in s`,
   422‚Üíthe cardinality stays the same because insert is idempotent.
   423‚Üí
   424‚Üí**Your task**: Given `a \\in s`, prove that `(insert a s).card = s.card`.
   425‚Üí"
   426‚Üí
   427‚Üí/-- Inserting an existing element doesn't change cardinality. -/
   428‚ÜíStatement (s : Finset ‚Ñï) (a : ‚Ñï) (ha : a ‚àà s) :
   429‚Üí    (insert a s).card = s.card := by
   430‚Üí  Hint "Since `a \\in s`, we know `insert a s = s` by
   431‚Üí  `Finset.insert_eq_of_mem`. Rewrite the goal using this."
   432‚Üí  Hint (hidden := true) "`rw [Finset.insert_eq_of_mem ha]`"
   433‚Üí  rw [Finset.insert_eq_of_mem ha]
   434‚Üí
   435‚ÜíConclusion "
   436‚ÜíOne line ‚Äî but the insight matters.
   437‚Üí
   438‚Üí`card_insert_of_notMem` says: if `a \\notin s`, then
   439‚Üí`(insert a s).card = s.card + 1`.
   440‚Üí
   441‚Üí`insert_eq_of_mem` says: if `a \\in s`, then
   442‚Üí`insert a s = s` (and therefore their cardinalities are equal).
   443‚Üí
   444‚ÜíTogether: inserting `a` either adds 1 (if `a` is new) or does
   445‚Üínothing (if `a` already exists). There's no middle case. This is
   446‚Üíwhy the non-membership hypothesis in `card_insert_of_notMem` is
   447‚Üíessential ‚Äî without it, you can't conclude the `+ 1`.
   448‚Üí
   449‚Üí**The takeaway**: Always check `a \\in s` vs `a \\notin s` before
   450‚Üíapplying `card_insert_of_notMem`. The wrong assumption leads to
   451‚Üían off-by-one error.
   452‚Üí"
   453‚Üí
   454‚ÜíTheoremTab "Card"
   455‚Üí
   456‚ÜíDisabledTactic trivial ¬´decide¬ª native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num by_cases tauto linarith
   457‚Üíimport Game.Metadata
   458‚Üí
   459‚ÜíWorld "Cardinality"
   460‚ÜíLevel 8
   461‚Üí
   462‚ÜíTitle "Computing a Concrete Cardinality"
   463‚Üí
   464‚ÜíIntroduction "
   465‚Üí# Chaining the Card Lemmas
   466‚Üí
   467‚ÜíYou have three tools:
   468‚Üí- `card_empty`: $|\\emptyset| = 0$
   469‚Üí- `card_singleton a`: $|\\{a\\}| = 1$
   470‚Üí- `card_insert_of_notMem h`: $|\\text{insert}(a, s)| = |s| + 1$ when $a \\notin s$
   471‚Üí
   472‚ÜíTo compute $|\\{1, 2, 3\\}|$, peel the finset layer by layer:
   473‚Üí
   474‚Üí$$\\{1, 2, 3\\} = \\text{insert}\\;1\\;\\{2, 3\\}$$
   475‚Üí
   476‚ÜíSo $|\\{1, 2, 3\\}| = |\\{2, 3\\}| + 1$ (since $1 \\notin \\{2, 3\\}$).
   477‚Üí
   478‚ÜíThen $\\{2, 3\\} = \\text{insert}\\;2\\;\\{3\\}$, so $|\\{2, 3\\}| = |\\{3\\}| + 1$.
   479‚Üí
   480‚ÜíFinally $|\\{3\\}| = 1$ by `card_singleton`.
   481‚Üí
   482‚ÜíEach `card_insert_of_notMem` step requires a non-membership proof.
   483‚ÜíYou'll prove these yourself using the membership pattern from
   484‚ÜíFinsetBasics: assume membership, rewrite with `mem_insert`/`mem_singleton`,
   485‚Üíand derive a contradiction.
   486‚Üí
   487‚Üí**Your task**: Prove $|\\{1, 2, 3\\}| = 3$ from scratch.
   488‚Üí"
   489‚Üí
   490‚Üí/-- {1, 2, 3} has exactly 3 elements. -/
   491‚ÜíStatement : ({1, 2, 3} : Finset ‚Ñï).card = 3 := by
   492‚Üí  Hint "First prove that 1 is not in the finset containing 2 and 3.
   493‚Üí  Use `have` to state it:
   494‚Üí  `have h1 : (1 : ‚Ñï) ‚àâ (insert 2 (singleton 3) : Finset ‚Ñï) := by ...`
   495‚Üí  (Recall that the finset notation for two elements is `insert` + `singleton`.)"
   496‚Üí  Hint (hidden := true) "Inside the `have`, use `intro h` to assume
   497‚Üí  membership, then `rw [Finset.mem_insert] at h` to get a disjunction.
   498‚Üí  Case-split with `cases h with | inl h => omega | inr h => ...`
   499‚Üí  and for the right branch, `rw [Finset.mem_singleton] at h; omega`."
   500‚Üí  have h1 : (1 : ‚Ñï) ‚àâ ({2, 3} : Finset ‚Ñï) := by
   501‚Üí    Hint "You need to show `1 ‚àâ insert 2 (singleton 3)`.
   502‚Üí    Assume membership with `intro h`."
   503‚Üí    intro h
   504‚Üí    Hint "Now `h : 1 ‚àà insert 2 (singleton 3)`.
   505‚Üí    Rewrite with `rw [Finset.mem_insert] at h` to get a disjunction."
   506‚Üí    rw [Finset.mem_insert] at h
   507‚Üí    Hint "Case-split on `h` with
   508‚Üí    `cases h with | inl h => omega | inr h => ...`"
   509‚Üí    cases h with
   510‚Üí    | inl h => omega
   511‚Üí    | inr h => rw [Finset.mem_singleton] at h; omega
   512‚Üí  Hint "Now prove that 2 is not in the singleton containing 3, the same way."
   513‚Üí  Hint (hidden := true) "`have h2 : (2 : ‚Ñï) ‚àâ (singleton 3 : Finset ‚Ñï) := by`
   514‚Üí  then `intro h; rw [Finset.mem_singleton] at h; omega`"
   515‚Üí  have h2 : (2 : ‚Ñï) ‚àâ ({3} : Finset ‚Ñï) := by
   516‚Üí    Hint "Assume membership with `intro h`, then rewrite and derive
   517‚Üí    a contradiction."
   518‚Üí    Hint (hidden := true) "`intro h; rw [Finset.mem_singleton] at h; omega`"
   519‚Üí    intro h
   520‚Üí    rw [Finset.mem_singleton] at h; omega
   521‚Üí  Hint "Now peel the insert layers using the non-membership proofs.
   522‚Üí  `rw [Finset.card_insert_of_notMem h1]`."
   523‚Üí  rw [Finset.card_insert_of_notMem h1]
   524‚Üí  Hint "Peel the next layer: `rw [Finset.card_insert_of_notMem h2]`."
   525‚Üí  rw [Finset.card_insert_of_notMem h2]
   526‚Üí  Hint "The innermost set is a singleton: `rw [Finset.card_singleton]`."
   527‚Üí  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `rw [Finset.card_singleton]`."
   528‚Üí  rw [Finset.card_singleton]
   529‚Üí
   530‚ÜíConclusion "
   531‚ÜíYou computed a concrete cardinality from scratch:
   532‚Üí
   533‚Üí1. Prove non-membership for each insert layer (retrieval from FinsetBasics)
   534‚Üí2. `rw [card_insert_of_notMem h]` ‚Äî peel each insert
   535‚Üí3. `rw [card_singleton]` ‚Äî resolve the innermost singleton
   536‚Üí
   537‚ÜíThis is the full recipe for computing $|\\{a_1, \\ldots, a_n\\}|$:
   538‚Üíprove $n - 1$ non-membership facts, apply `card_insert` that many
   539‚Üítimes, then `card_singleton` once. The arithmetic resolves automatically.
   540‚Üí
   541‚ÜíThe non-membership proofs followed the FinsetBasics pattern:
   542‚Üíassume membership, rewrite with `mem_insert`/`mem_singleton`,
   543‚Üíand derive a contradiction with `omega`.
   544‚Üí"

   553‚Üí
   554‚ÜíTitle "Comparing Range Sizes"
   555‚Üí
   556‚ÜíIntroduction "
   557‚Üí# Cardinality of Range
   558‚Üí
   559‚ÜíYou learned `Finset.card_range` in the Finset Problem Set:
   560‚Üí
   561‚Üí$$|\\text{range}(n)| = n$$
   562‚Üí
   563‚ÜíIn Lean: `Finset.card_range n : (Finset.range n).card = n`
   564‚Üí
   565‚ÜíThis level puts it to work: use `card_range` to determine two
   566‚Üírange sizes, then let the arithmetic resolve the comparison.
   567‚Üí
   568‚Üí**Your task**: Prove that `range 10` has 3 more elements than `range 7`.
   569‚Üí"
   570‚Üí
   571‚Üí/-- range 10 has 3 more elements than range 7. -/
   572‚ÜíStatement : (Finset.range 10).card = (Finset.range 7).card + 3 := by
   573‚Üí  Hint "Apply `Finset.card_range` to both sides. You can chain two
   574‚Üí  rewrites: `rw [Finset.card_range, Finset.card_range]`."
   575‚Üí  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `rw [Finset.card_range, Finset.card_range]`.
   576‚Üí  After both rewrites the goal reduces to `10 = 7 + 3`, which Lean
   577‚Üí  closes automatically."
   578‚Üí  rw [Finset.card_range, Finset.card_range]
   579‚Üí
   580‚ÜíConclusion "
   581‚Üí`Finset.card_range n` turns abstract range cardinalities into concrete
   582‚Üínumbers. Once both sides are numerical, Lean's kernel reduction handles
   583‚Üíthe arithmetic.
   584‚Üí
   585‚ÜíYou first met `card_range` in PsetFinset Level 9. It will continue to
   586‚Üíappear whenever finset cardinality meets natural number indexing.
   587‚Üí
   588‚Üí**Coming up**: The next level introduces the `have` + `omega` pattern
   589‚Üí‚Äî bringing a card lemma into context with `have`, then using `omega`
   590‚Üífor the arithmetic. This is the main proof strategy for the rest of
   591‚Üíthe world.
   592‚Üí"
   593‚Üí
   594‚ÜíTheoremTab "Card"
   595‚Üí
   596‚ÜíDisabledTactic trivial ¬´decide¬ª native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num by_cases tauto linarith
   597‚Üíimport Game.Metadata
   598‚Üí
   599‚ÜíWorld "Cardinality"
   600‚ÜíLevel 10
   601‚Üí
   602‚ÜíTitle "Erase Removes One"
   603‚Üí
   604‚ÜíIntroduction "
   605‚Üí# Removing an Element
   606‚Üí
   607‚Üí`insert` adds one element and increases the count by 1. The dual
   608‚Üíoperation is `Finset.erase`: remove one element, and the count
   609‚Üídecreases by 1.
   610‚Üí
   611‚Üí$$a \\in s \\implies |s.\\text{erase}\\;a| = |s| - 1$$
   612‚Üí
   613‚ÜíIn Lean:
   614‚Üí```
   615‚ÜíFinset.card_erase_of_mem : a ‚àà s ‚Üí (s.erase a).card = s.card - 1
   616‚Üí```
   617‚Üí
   618‚ÜíThe hypothesis `a ‚àà s` is required ‚Äî if `a ‚àâ s`, then erasing it
   619‚Üídoes nothing.
   620‚Üí
   621‚ÜíNote the subtraction is on natural numbers. Since `.card` returns a
   622‚Üí`‚Ñï`, we use natural number subtraction (which floors at 0). When
   623‚Üí`a ‚àà s`, we know `s.card ‚â• 1`, so `s.card - 1` behaves as expected.
   624‚Üí
   625‚Üí**Your task**: Given `a ‚àà s` and `s.card = 5`, prove that erasing `a`
   626‚Üíleaves 4 elements.
   627‚Üí"
   628‚Üí
   629‚Üí/-- Erasing a member from a 5-element set leaves 4 elements. -/
   630‚ÜíStatement (s : Finset ‚Ñï) (a : ‚Ñï) (h : a ‚àà s) (hs : s.card = 5) :
   631‚Üí    (s.erase a).card = 4 := by
   632‚Üí  Hint "First, use `Finset.card_erase_of_mem h` to get the general
   633‚Üí  equation: `(s.erase a).card = s.card - 1`."
   634‚Üí  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `have he := Finset.card_erase_of_mem h`
   635‚Üí  to add the equation to your context. Then use `omega` to combine
   636‚Üí  it with `hs`."
   637‚Üí  have he := Finset.card_erase_of_mem h
   638‚Üí  Hint "Now you have `he : (s.erase a).card = s.card - 1` and
   639‚Üí  `hs : s.card = 5`. The arithmetic `5 - 1 = 4` is handled by `omega`."
   640‚Üí  omega
   641‚Üí
   642‚ÜíConclusion "
   643‚ÜíThe `have` + `omega` pattern is powerful for cardinality reasoning:
   644‚Üí1. Use `have` to bring a card lemma into the context
   645‚Üí2. Use `omega` to handle the arithmetic
   646‚Üí
   647‚ÜíThis is better than trying to `rw` card lemmas directly into the
   648‚Üígoal, because `omega` can combine multiple arithmetic facts at once.
   649‚ÜíYou'll use this pattern repeatedly in the remaining levels.
   650‚Üí
   651‚Üí`card_erase_of_mem` is the complement of `card_insert_of_notMem`:
   652‚Üí- Insert a new element: size goes up by 1
   653‚Üí- Erase an existing element: size goes down by 1
   654‚Üí"
   655‚Üí
   656‚Üí/-- `Finset.card_erase_of_mem h` states that when `h : a ‚àà s`,
   657‚Üí`(s.erase a).card = s.card - 1`.
   658‚Üí
   659‚Üí## Syntax
   660‚Üí```
   661‚Üíhave he := Finset.card_erase_of_mem h
   662‚Üí```
   663‚Üí
   664‚Üí## When to use it
   665‚ÜíWhen you need to relate the cardinality of `s.erase a` to `s.card`,
   666‚Üíknowing that `a ‚àà s`.
   667‚Üí
   668‚Üí## Warning
   669‚ÜíThe subtraction is over `‚Ñï`, so `s.card - 1` is natural number
   670‚Üísubtraction (which floors at 0). When `a ‚àà s`, we know `s.card ‚â• 1`,
   671‚Üíso this subtraction is exact.
   672‚Üí-/

   682‚ÜíLevel 11
   683‚Üí
   684‚ÜíTitle "Subsets Can't Be Bigger"
   685‚Üí
   686‚ÜíIntroduction "
   687‚Üí# Monotonicity of Cardinality
   688‚Üí
   689‚ÜíIf every element of `s` is also in `t`, then `s` can't have more
   690‚Üíelements than `t`:
   691‚Üí
   692‚Üí$$s \\subseteq t \\implies |s| \\leq |t|$$
   693‚Üí
   694‚ÜíIn Lean:
   695‚Üí```
   696‚ÜíFinset.card_le_card : s ‚äÜ t ‚Üí s.card ‚â§ t.card
   697‚Üí```
   698‚Üí
   699‚ÜíThis is **monotonicity**: cardinality respects the subset relation.

/-- `Finset.card_eq_zero` states that `s.card = 0 ‚Üî s = ‚àÖ`.

A finset has zero elements if and only if it is the empty set.

## Syntax
```
exact Finset.card_eq_zero.mp hs  -- from s.card = 0 to s = ‚àÖ
rw [‚Üê Finset.card_eq_zero]      -- convert goal s = ‚àÖ to s.card = 0
```

## When to use it
When you know `s.card = 0` and need `s = ‚àÖ`, or vice versa.
-/

import Game.Metadata

World "Cardinality"
Level 6

Title "Insert Adds One"

Introduction "
# Inserting a New Element

If `a` is **not** already in `s`, then inserting `a` increases the
cardinality by exactly one:

$a \\notin s \\implies |\\text{insert}(a, s)| = |s| + 1$

In Lean:
```
Finset.card_insert_of_notMem : a ‚àâ s ‚Üí (insert a s).card = s.card + 1
```

The hypothesis `a ‚àâ s` is essential. If `a` were already in `s`, then
`insert a s = s` (by the idempotency you saw in FinsetBasics), and
the cardinality wouldn't change. The theorem requires you to *prove*
the element is new before it promises the count goes up.

**Your task**: Given that `a ‚àâ s`, prove that inserting `a` increases
the cardinality by 1.
"

/-- Inserting a new element increases cardinality by 1. -/
Statement (s : Finset ‚Ñï) (a : ‚Ñï) (h : a ‚àâ s) :
    (insert a s).card = s.card + 1 := by
  Hint "You have `h : a ‚àâ s` and need to show `(insert a s).card = s.card + 1`.
  This is exactly `Finset.card_insert_of_notMem h`."
  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `exact Finset.card_insert_of_notMem h`."
  exact Finset.card_insert_of_notMem h

Conclusion "
`Finset.card_insert_of_notMem` is the inductive step for cardinality.
Combined with the base cases (`card_empty`, `card_singleton`), it lets
you compute the cardinality of any finset built by iterated insertion:

$$|\\emptyset| = 0, \\quad |\\{a\\}| = 1, \\quad |\\text{insert}(a, s)| = |s| + 1 \\text{ (if } a \\notin s\\text{)}$$

The non-membership hypothesis is the critical ingredient ‚Äî without it,
the lemma doesn't apply. The next level puts this to work: computing
the cardinality of a concrete finset by chaining `card_insert` steps.
"

/-- `Finset.card_insert_of_notMem h` states that when `h : a ‚àâ s`,
`(insert a s).card = s.card + 1`.

## Syntax
```
exact Finset.card_insert_of_notMem h
rw [Finset.card_insert_of_notMem h]
```

## When to use it
When the goal involves the cardinality of `insert a s` and you know
(or can prove) that `a ‚àâ s`.

## Important
The hypothesis `a ‚àâ s` is required. If `a ‚àà s`, then `insert a s = s`
and the cardinality does not change.

## Example
```
-- h : 5 ‚àâ s
-- Goal: (insert 5 s).card = s.card + 1
exact Finset.card_insert_of_notMem h
```
-/

import Game.Metadata

World "Cardinality"
Level 7

Title "Insert Doesn't Always Add One"

Introduction "
# When Insert is a No-Op

In Level 6, you learned `card_insert_of_notMem`: inserting a NEW
element adds 1 to the cardinality. But what if the element is
ALREADY in the set?

You proved in FinsetBasics that `Finset.insert_eq_of_mem`:
if `a \\in s`, then `insert a s = s` ‚Äî insert is a no-op.

The cardinality consequence: if `a \\in s`, then
`(insert a s).card = s.card` ‚Äî the count doesn't change.

This addresses a natural misconception: `card(insert a s) = card s + 1`
is NOT always true. It's only true when `a \\notin s`. When `a \\in s`,
the cardinality stays the same because insert is idempotent.

**Your task**: Given `a \\in s`, prove that `(insert a s).card = s.card`.
"

/-- Inserting an existing element doesn't change cardinality. -/
Statement (s : Finset ‚Ñï) (a : ‚Ñï) (ha : a ‚àà s) :
    (insert a s).card = s.card := by
  Hint "Since `a \\in s`, we know `insert a s = s` by
  `Finset.insert_eq_of_mem`. Rewrite the goal using this."
  Hint (hidden := true) "`rw [Finset.insert_eq_of_mem ha]`"
  rw [Finset.insert_eq_of_mem ha]

Conclusion "
One line ‚Äî but the insight matters.

`card_insert_of_notMem` says: if `a \\notin s`, then
`(insert a s).card = s.card + 1`.

`insert_eq_of_mem` says: if `a \\in s`, then
`insert a s = s` (and therefore their cardinalities are equal).

Together: inserting `a` either adds 1 (if `a` is new) or does
nothing (if `a` already exists). There's no middle case. This is
why the non-membership hypothesis in `card_insert_of_notMem` is
essential ‚Äî without it, you can't conclude the `+ 1`.

**The takeaway**: Always check `a \\in s` vs `a \\notin s` before
applying `card_insert_of_notMem`. The wrong assumption leads to
an off-by-one error.
"

import Game.Metadata

World "Cardinality"
Level 8

Title "Computing a Concrete Cardinality"

Introduction "
# Chaining the Card Lemmas

You have three tools:
- `card_empty`: $|\\emptyset| = 0$
- `card_singleton a`: $|\\{a\\}| = 1$
- `card_insert_of_notMem h`: $|\\text{insert}(a, s)| = |s| + 1$ when $a \\notin s$

To compute $|\\{1, 2, 3\\}|$, peel the finset layer by layer:

$$\\{1, 2, 3\\} = \\text{insert}\\;1\\;\\{2, 3\\}$$

So $|\\{1, 2, 3\\}| = |\\{2, 3\\}| + 1$ (since $1 \\notin \\{2, 3\\}$).

Then $\\{2, 3\\} = \\text{insert}\\;2\\;\\{3\\}$, so $|\\{2, 3\\}| = |\\{3\\}| + 1$.

Finally $|\\{3\\}| = 1$ by `card_singleton`.

Each `card_insert_of_notMem` step requires a non-membership proof.
You'll prove these yourself using the membership pattern from
FinsetBasics: assume membership, rewrite with `mem_insert`/`mem_singleton`,
and derive a contradiction.

**Your task**: Prove $|\\{1, 2, 3\\}| = 3$ from scratch.
"

/-- {1, 2, 3} has exactly 3 elements. -/
Statement : ({1, 2, 3} : Finset ‚Ñï).card = 3 := by
  Hint "First prove that 1 is not in the finset containing 2 and 3.
  Use `have` to state it:
  `have h1 : (1 : ‚Ñï) ‚àâ (insert 2 (singleton 3) : Finset ‚Ñï) := by ...`
  (Recall that the finset notation for two elements is `insert` + `singleton`.)"
  Hint (hidden := true) "Inside the `have`, use `intro h` to assume
  membership, then `rw [Finset.mem_insert] at h` to get a disjunction.
  Case-split with `cases h with | inl h => omega | inr h => ...`
  and for the right branch, `rw [Finset.mem_singleton] at h; omega`."
  have h1 : (1 : ‚Ñï) ‚àâ ({2, 3} : Finset ‚Ñï) := by
    Hint "You need to show `1 ‚àâ insert 2 (singleton 3)`.
    Assume membership with `intro h`."
    intro h
    Hint "Now `h : 1 ‚àà insert 2 (singleton 3)`.
    Rewrite with `rw [Finset.mem_insert] at h` to get a disjunction."
    rw [Finset.mem_insert] at h
    Hint "Case-split on `h` with
    `cases h with | inl h => omega | inr h => ...`"
    cases h with
    | inl h => omega
    | inr h => rw [Finset.mem_singleton] at h; omega
  Hint "Now prove that 2 is not in the singleton containing 3, the same way."
  Hint (hidden := true) "`have h2 : (2 : ‚Ñï) ‚àâ (singleton 3 : Finset ‚Ñï) := by`
  then `intro h; rw [Finset.mem_singleton] at h; omega`"
  have h2 : (2 : ‚Ñï) ‚àâ ({3} : Finset ‚Ñï) := by
    Hint "Assume membership with `intro h`, then rewrite and derive
    a contradiction."
    Hint (hidden := true) "`intro h; rw [Finset.mem_singleton] at h; omega`"
    intro h
    rw [Finset.mem_singleton] at h; omega
  Hint "Now peel the insert layers using the non-membership proofs.
  `rw [Finset.card_insert_of_notMem h1]`."
  rw [Finset.card_insert_of_notMem h1]
  Hint "Peel the next layer: `rw [Finset.card_insert_of_notMem h2]`."
  rw [Finset.card_insert_of_notMem h2]
  Hint "The innermost set is a singleton: `rw [Finset.card_singleton]`."
  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `rw [Finset.card_singleton]`."
  rw [Finset.card_singleton]

Conclusion "
You computed a concrete cardinality from scratch:

1. Prove non-membership for each insert layer (retrieval from FinsetBasics)
2. `rw [card_insert_of_notMem h]` ‚Äî peel each insert
3. `rw [card_singleton]` ‚Äî resolve the innermost singleton

This is the full recipe for computing $|\\{a_1, \\ldots, a_n\\}|$:
prove $n - 1$ non-membership facts, apply `card_insert` that many
times, then `card_singleton` once. The arithmetic resolves automatically.

The non-membership proofs followed the FinsetBasics pattern:
assume membership, rewrite with `mem_insert`/`mem_singleton`,
and derive a contradiction with `omega`.
"

Level 9

Title "Comparing Range Sizes"

Introduction "
# Cardinality of Range

You learned `Finset.card_range` in the Finset Problem Set:

$$|\\text{range}(n)| = n$$

In Lean: `Finset.card_range n : (Finset.range n).card = n`

This level puts it to work: use `card_range` to determine two
range sizes, then let the arithmetic resolve the comparison.

**Your task**: Prove that `range 10` has 3 more elements than `range 7`.
"

/-- range 10 has 3 more elements than range 7. -/
Statement : (Finset.range 10).card = (Finset.range 7).card + 3 := by
  Hint "Apply `Finset.card_range` to both sides. You can chain two
  rewrites: `rw [Finset.card_range, Finset.card_range]`."
  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `rw [Finset.card_range, Finset.card_range]`.
  After both rewrites the goal reduces to `10 = 7 + 3`, which Lean
  closes automatically."
  rw [Finset.card_range, Finset.card_range]

Conclusion "
`Finset.card_range n` turns abstract range cardinalities into concrete
numbers. Once both sides are numerical, Lean's kernel reduction handles
the arithmetic.

You first met `card_range` in PsetFinset Level 9. It will continue to
appear whenever finset cardinality meets natural number indexing.

**Coming up**: The next level introduces the `have` + `omega` pattern
‚Äî bringing a card lemma into context with `have`, then using `omega`
for the arithmetic. This is the main proof strategy for the rest of
the world.
"

World "Cardinality"
Level 10

Title "Erase Removes One"

Introduction "
# Removing an Element

`insert` adds one element and increases the count by 1. The dual
operation is `Finset.erase`: remove one element, and the count
decreases by 1.

$$a \\in s \\implies |s.\\text{erase}\\;a| = |s| - 1$$

In Lean:
```
Finset.card_erase_of_mem : a ‚àà s ‚Üí (s.erase a).card = s.card - 1
```

The hypothesis `a ‚àà s` is required ‚Äî if `a ‚àâ s`, then erasing it
does nothing.

Note the subtraction is on natural numbers. Since `.card` returns a
`‚Ñï`, we use natural number subtraction (which floors at 0). When
`a ‚àà s`, we know `s.card ‚â• 1`, so `s.card - 1` behaves as expected.

**Your task**: Given `a ‚àà s` and `s.card = 5`, prove that erasing `a`
leaves 4 elements.
"

/-- Erasing a member from a 5-element set leaves 4 elements. -/
Statement (s : Finset ‚Ñï) (a : ‚Ñï) (h : a ‚àà s) (hs : s.card = 5) :
    (s.erase a).card = 4 := by
  Hint "First, use `Finset.card_erase_of_mem h` to get the general
  equation: `(s.erase a).card = s.card - 1`."
  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `have he := Finset.card_erase_of_mem h`
  to add the equation to your context. Then use `omega` to combine
  it with `hs`."
  have he := Finset.card_erase_of_mem h
  Hint "Now you have `he : (s.erase a).card = s.card - 1` and
  `hs : s.card = 5`. The arithmetic `5 - 1 = 4` is handled by `omega`."
  omega

Conclusion "
The `have` + `omega` pattern is powerful for cardinality reasoning:
1. Use `have` to bring a card lemma into the context
2. Use `omega` to handle the arithmetic

This is better than trying to `rw` card lemmas directly into the
goal, because `omega` can combine multiple arithmetic facts at once.
You'll use this pattern repeatedly in the remaining levels.

`card_erase_of_mem` is the complement of `card_insert_of_notMem`:
- Insert a new element: size goes up by 1
- Erase an existing element: size goes down by 1
"

/-- `Finset.card_erase_of_mem h` states that when `h : a ‚àà s`,
`(s.erase a).card = s.card - 1`.

## Syntax
```
have he := Finset.card_erase_of_mem h
```

## When to use it
When you need to relate the cardinality of `s.erase a` to `s.card`,
knowing that `a ‚àà s`.

## Warning
The subtraction is over `‚Ñï`, so `s.card - 1` is natural number
subtraction (which floors at 0). When `a ‚àà s`, we know `s.card ‚â• 1`,
so this subtraction is exact.
-/

World "Cardinality"
Level 11

Title "Subsets Can't Be Bigger"

Introduction "
# Monotonicity of Cardinality

If every element of `s` is also in `t`, then `s` can't have more
elements than `t`:

$$s \\subseteq t \\implies |s| \\leq |t|$$

In Lean:
```
Finset.card_le_card : s ‚äÜ t ‚Üí s.card ‚â§ t.card
```

This is **monotonicity**: cardinality respects the subset relation.

content.content:
300‚Üí
   301‚Üí`List.length_append : (l‚ÇÅ ++ l‚ÇÇ).length = l‚ÇÅ.length + l‚ÇÇ.length`
   302‚Üí
   303‚ÜíThis is the list-level version of the addition principle: the length
   304‚Üíof a concatenation is the sum of the lengths.
   305‚Üí
   306‚Üí**Your task**: Given two lists with known lengths, determine the length
   307‚Üíof their concatenation.
   308‚Üí"
   309‚Üí
   310‚Üí/-- The length of a concatenation equals the sum of the lengths. -/
   311‚ÜíStatement (l‚ÇÅ l‚ÇÇ : List ‚Ñï) (h‚ÇÅ : l‚ÇÅ.length = 3) (h‚ÇÇ : l‚ÇÇ.length = 4) :
   312‚Üí    (l‚ÇÅ ++ l‚ÇÇ).length = 7 := by
   313‚Üí  Hint "Start by rewriting with `List.length_append` to split the
   314‚Üí  concatenation length into a sum."
   315‚Üí  rw [List.length_append]
   316‚Üí  Hint "Now the goal is `l‚ÇÅ.length + l‚ÇÇ.length = 7`. Rewrite with
   317‚Üí  the hypotheses `h‚ÇÅ` and `h‚ÇÇ`."
   318‚Üí  rw [h‚ÇÅ, h‚ÇÇ]
   319‚Üí
   320‚ÜíConclusion "
   321‚Üí`List.length_append` is the additive decomposition for list length.
   322‚ÜíCombined with `length_cons` and `length_nil`, you can compute the
   323‚Üílength of any list expression.
   324‚Üí
   325‚ÜíIn plain math: $|l_1 \\cdot l_2| = |l_1| + |l_2|$ where $\\cdot$ is
   326‚Üíconcatenation.
   327‚Üí
   328‚Üí**Why this matters for the ladder**: When we quotient lists to get
   329‚Üímultisets, this length fact becomes `Multiset.card_add`. The
   330‚Üíarithmetic is preserved even though the ordering is forgotten.
   331‚Üí"
   332‚Üí
   333‚Üí/-- `List.length_append` states that
   334‚Üí`(l‚ÇÅ ++ l‚ÇÇ).length = l‚ÇÅ.length + l‚ÇÇ.length`.

   341‚Üí## When to use it
   342‚ÜíWhen the goal involves the length of an appended list `(l‚ÇÅ ++ l‚ÇÇ).length`.

   354‚Üí
   355‚ÜíTitle "Duplicate Elements"
   356‚Üí
   357‚ÜíIntroduction "
   358‚Üí# Lists Can Have Duplicates
   359‚Üí
   360‚ÜíBefore we move to permutations, let's observe something important:
   361‚Üí**lists can contain duplicate elements**.
   362‚Üí
   363‚ÜíConsider a list `l` where element `a` already appears (`a ‚àà l`).
   364‚ÜíIf you prepend `a` to `l` with `a :: l`, the resulting list has
   365‚Üí`a` appearing at least twice ‚Äî once at the head, and at least once
   366‚Üíin the tail.
   367‚Üí
   368‚ÜíThis is a preview of the abstraction ladder:
   369‚Üí
   370‚Üí| Layer | Duplicates? | Count measure |
   371‚Üí|---|---|---|
   372‚Üí| **List** | yes | `.length` counts all entries |
   373‚Üí| **Multiset** | yes | `.card` counts all entries |
   374‚Üí| **Finset** | no | `.card` counts distinct elements |
   375‚Üí
   376‚ÜíLists and multisets keep every copy. Finsets discard duplicates,
   377‚Üíso their `.card` can be strictly smaller than the list `.length`.
   378‚Üí
   379‚Üí**Your task**: Given a list `l` of length 1 and an element `a`
   380‚Üíthat belongs to `l`, compute the length of `a :: b :: l`.
   381‚Üí
   382‚ÜíSince `a ‚àà l`, the list `a :: b :: l` contains `a` at least twice.
   383‚ÜíBut `.length` counts all entries ‚Äî duplicates included. You'll peel
   384‚Üíoff each `cons` with `List.length_cons` and close with the hypothesis.
   385‚Üí"
   386‚Üí
   387‚Üí/-- Prepending two elements to a list increases its length by 2. -/
   388‚ÜíStatement (a b : ‚Ñï) (l : List ‚Ñï) (hmem : a ‚àà l) (hlen : l.length = 1) :
   389‚Üí    (a :: b :: l).length = 3 := by
   390‚Üí  Hint "The outermost structure is `a :: (b :: l)`. Use
   391‚Üí  `rw [List.length_cons]` to peel off `a`."
   392‚Üí  rw [List.length_cons]
   393‚Üí  Hint "Good! The goal is now `(b :: l).length + 1 = 3`. Peel off
   394‚Üí  `b` with another `List.length_cons`."
   395‚Üí  rw [List.length_cons]
   396‚Üí  Hint "The goal is now `l.length + 1 + 1 = 3`. Use the hypothesis
   397‚Üí  `hlen : l.length = 1` to substitute."
   398‚Üí  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `rw [hlen]`."
   399‚Üí  rw [hlen]
   400‚Üí
   401‚ÜíConclusion "
   402‚ÜíYou computed the length by peeling off each `cons` with
   403‚Üí`List.length_cons`, then closing with the known tail length.
   404‚Üí
   405‚ÜíThe pattern:
   406‚Üí```
   407‚Üí(a :: b :: l).length = (b :: l).length + 1    (length_cons)
   408‚Üí                     = l.length + 1 + 1        (length_cons)
   409‚Üí                     = 1 + 1 + 1               (hypothesis)
   410‚Üí                     = 3
   411‚Üí```
   412‚Üí
   413‚ÜíThe hypothesis `hmem : a ‚àà l` wasn't needed for the length
   414‚Üícalculation ‚Äî `.length` counts all entries regardless of duplicates.
   415‚ÜíBut it matters *conceptually*: the list `a :: b :: l` contains `a`
   416‚Üíat least twice (once at the head, once in `l`). This means:
   417‚Üí- As a **list**: `.length = 3` (counts everything)
   418‚Üí- As a **finset**: `.card` could be 2 or 3 (depending on `b`)
   419‚Üí
   420‚ÜíLater in this world, you'll see exactly how the nodup condition
   421‚Üídetermines whether card equals length.
   422‚Üí"
   423‚Üí

   432‚ÜíTitle "Permutations"
   433‚Üí
   434‚ÜíIntroduction "
   435‚Üí# Lists Are Ordered ‚Äî Permutations Ignore Order
   436‚Üí
   437‚ÜíTwo lists with the same elements in different orders are **not** equal:
   438‚Üí`[1, 2, 3] ‚âÝ [2, 1, 3]`. Order is part of a list's identity.
   439‚Üí
   440‚ÜíBut sometimes we want to say 'these lists have the same elements,
   441‚Üíjust rearranged.' That's `List.Perm` ‚Äî the permutation relation.
   442‚Üí
   443‚ÜíWe write `l‚ÇÅ ~ l‚ÇÇ` (or `l‚ÇÅ.Perm l‚ÇÇ`) to mean `l‚ÇÅ` is a permutation
   444‚Üíof `l‚ÇÇ`.
   445‚Üí
   446‚ÜíThe key building blocks:
   447‚Üí
   448‚Üí- **`List.Perm.swap x y l`** ‚Äî swapping two adjacent elements gives
   449‚Üí  a permutation: `[y, x] ++ l ~ [x, y] ++ l`
   450‚Üí- **`List.Perm.cons a h`** ‚Äî if `l‚ÇÅ ~ l‚ÇÇ`, then `a :: l‚ÇÅ ~ a :: l‚ÇÇ`
   451‚Üí  (prepending the same element preserves the permutation)
   452‚Üí- **`List.Perm.trans h‚ÇÅ h‚ÇÇ`** ‚Äî if `l‚ÇÅ ~ l‚ÇÇ` and `l‚ÇÇ ~ l‚ÇÉ`,
   453‚Üí  then `l‚ÇÅ ~ l‚ÇÉ` (chain permutations)
   454‚Üí
   455‚Üí**Your task**: Prove that `[2, 1, 3]` is a permutation of `[1, 2, 3]`.
   456‚ÜíThis is a single swap of the first two elements.
   457‚Üí"
   458‚Üí
   459‚Üí/-- Swapping the first two elements gives a permutation. -/
   460‚ÜíStatement : List.Perm [2, 1, 3] [1, 2, 3] := by
   461‚Üí  Hint "The lists `[2, 1, 3]` and `[1, 2, 3]` differ only in the order
   462‚Üí  of the first two elements. This is exactly `List.Perm.swap 1 2 [3]`."
   463‚Üí  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `exact List.Perm.swap 1 2 [3]`.
   464‚Üí  This says: swapping `1` and `2` in front of `[3]` gives
   465‚Üí  `[2, 1, 3] ~ [1, 2, 3]`."
   466‚Üí  exact List.Perm.swap 1 2 [3]
   467‚Üí
   468‚ÜíConclusion "
   469‚Üí`List.Perm.swap` is the atomic permutation move: swap two adjacent
   470‚Üíelements. Every permutation can be built from a sequence of swaps.
   471‚Üí
   472‚ÜíThe type signature: `List.Perm.swap x y l : (y :: x :: l).Perm (x :: y :: l)`.
   473‚Üí
   474‚ÜíNote the argument order: `swap x y l` produces `[y, x, ...] ~ [x, y, ...]`.
   475‚ÜíThe elements appear **reversed** on the left side.
   476‚Üí
   477‚Üí**Why permutations matter for the ladder**: The `Multiset` type is
   478‚Üídefined as the quotient `List Œ± / Perm`. Two lists that are
   479‚Üípermutations of each other become the **same** multiset. This is
   480‚Üíhow order gets forgotten.
   481‚Üí"
   482‚Üí
   483‚Üí/-- `List.Perm` is the permutation relation on lists. `l‚ÇÅ.Perm l‚ÇÇ`
   484‚Üí(notation: `l‚ÇÅ ~ l‚ÇÇ`) means `l‚ÇÅ` is a rearrangement of `l‚ÇÇ`.
   485‚Üí
   486‚Üí## Building permutations
   487‚Üí- `List.Perm.swap x y l : (y :: x :: l).Perm (x :: y :: l)` ‚Äî swap adjacent
   488‚Üí- `List.Perm.cons a h : (a :: l‚ÇÅ).Perm (a :: l‚ÇÇ)` from `h : l‚ÇÅ.Perm l‚ÇÇ`
   489‚Üí- `List.Perm.trans h‚ÇÅ h‚ÇÇ` ‚Äî chain two permutations
   490‚Üí
   491‚Üí## Key facts
   492‚Üí- `List.Perm.length_eq : l‚ÇÅ.Perm l‚ÇÇ ‚Üí l‚ÇÅ.length = l‚ÇÇ.length`
   493‚Üí- `Multiset.coe_eq_coe : ‚Üël‚ÇÅ = ‚Üël‚ÇÇ ‚Üî l‚ÇÅ.Perm l‚ÇÇ`
   494‚Üí-/
   495‚ÜíDefinitionDoc List.Perm as "List.Perm"
   496‚Üí
   497‚Üí/-- `List.Perm.swap x y l` proves `(y :: x :: l).Perm (x :: y :: l)`.
   498‚Üí
   499‚Üí## Syntax
   500‚Üí```
   501‚Üíexact List.Perm.swap x y l
   502‚Üí```
   503‚Üí
   504‚Üí## When to use it
   505‚ÜíWhen two lists differ only by a swap of two adjacent elements.
   506‚Üí
   507‚Üí## Warning
   508‚ÜíThe arguments `x y` appear in the *reverse* order on the left:
   509‚Üí`swap x y l` gives `[y, x, ...] ~ [x, y, ...]`.
   510‚Üí-/
   511‚ÜíTheoremDoc List.Perm.swap as "List.Perm.swap" in "List"
   512‚Üí
   513‚Üí/-- `List.Perm.cons a h` proves `(a :: l‚ÇÅ).Perm (a :: l‚ÇÇ)` from
   514‚Üí`h : l‚ÇÅ.Perm l‚ÇÇ`.
   515‚Üí
   516‚Üí## Syntax
   517‚Üí```
   518‚Üíexact List.Perm.cons a h
   519‚Üí```
   520‚Üí
   521‚Üí## When to use it
   522‚ÜíWhen two lists have the same head and their tails are permutations.
   523‚Üí-/
   524‚ÜíTheoremDoc List.Perm.cons as "List.Perm.cons" in "List"
